sentences.text: No, I didn't.
I really never did.
So I once had an incident in the factory, which was very scary, because while I was working on this little machine making the shells for the bullet, I was doing fine.
And then one day, a foreman came and said-- took me away from that machine and put me on another machine.
It was a big press in the middle of the factory.
And that press actually appeared in the movie.
And it, it was very difficult.
I think there was-- had to-- it was coming up and down, but you had to push it up and down.
And I don't even know-- don't remember what it was making, but it was hard to operate.
And everybody had problems.
That machine was always breaking.
And while I was working there, the machine broke down.
And the foreman came running and yelling that I sabotaged the production.
Well, sentence for sabotage was death, you know.
They could kill you.
And somebody went to get Oskar Schindler, and he came down.
And I remember how he looked at the machine.
Now again, he was always walk with the cigar.
And he was always so beautifully dressed in double breasted suits.
I mean, you know, we were all dirty, and then we work rags, and here come-- and then there were the soldiers.
And here comes Oskar Schindler.
Every day he wore a new suit and new shirt.
And he-- I mean, he was like, he was hope.
You know, he was life.
He was maybe future.
He was, he was God for us.
And never a bad word to anyone.
And I remember how he came down.
And he, he came down.
And he said, [GERMAN], what's happening.
And they say to him that I, I, I did something to the machine, I broke the machine.
And he looks and walks around.
He looks at me.
He looks at the foreman.
I mean, his face, and he says, this little girl broke this machine?
He says, he says, you moron.
She can't-- she couldn't possibly break the machine.
He says, you fix that machine, and don't ever put anybody small like that and weak like that on the machine like that.
You have to-- only men is allowed to work on the machine.
And of course I was crying.
And he said to me-- he patted me on my head and he said, don't worry, [GERMAN].
And he asked me, where were you working before?
And I painted-- I pointed this machine to him.
And he said to the foreman, that's what she works, that's where she's going to stay.
And then, of course, I was afraid the foreman will take it out on me.
But he wouldn't, he wouldn't dare.
He wouldn't dare, because Oskar Schindler, he used to come to the factory every day.
So every day he would come to the factory, he would pat me on my-- on the head.
And he would say, [GERMAN], you know, how you doing, little one?
He made everybody, you know, protected.
He was so good to everybody.
Who was the foreman?
What--
I don't remember.
He was German.
He was German.
I don't know who he was.
And most of the foremans that we had were Jewish, and there was some Polish.
So I don't know who-- you know, I don't remember that much about him that-- except when that machine broke down and I see him coming at me.
I remember that.
The people in the factory, they were, they were OK.
But they were-- actually, most of the Jewish people were the foremen.
And I always had a boss who was Jewish.
So I don't know if that day that I was put on that machine if somebody was visiting and something happened.
I don't know.
I don't remember.
But Oskar Schindler, when somebody was sick, he would try to help them.
I-- well, my mother had problems.
Mostly because-- well, we had all kinds of problems from malnutrition, you know.
I remember I had terrible sores on my hands and my legs.
And my nails, you know.
I would always get an infection on my nails, and the nails would come off.
And I had pneumonia when I was in Plaszow.
I was sick.
I was there for three weeks.
I was awfully sick.
And again, my cousin, the one who was killed, he used to come and bring me lemons to the-- and then they took me out.
They were afraid to leave me because they had-- they were going to come and take all the sick people from the, from the clinic.
So they took me out.
And so we were always sick, because we didn't have enough-- my mother had problems, her gums, you know, it was her teeth.
She had infection.
And it was-- you know, we were starving.
Even, even working for Oskar Schindler, we had much more food than people in Plaszow.
Just the rations were bigger.
And just the, the soups, there was meat in them, which, you know, in Plaszow we didn't have.
So you could survive in Oskar Schindler's camp.
I, I was there from the beginning from-- I was there in 1944.
I think that we probably went there in 1943, towards the-- maybe in December, November, December.
And then we stayed there until 1944 in, in August, late August.
And then we came to him and sent back to Plaszow because the camp was being liquidated.
And Oskar Schindler said he was going to move his camp to the factory to bring it to Czechoslovakia, and he was going to take us.
He again, we had alll-- he assembled us all in a courtyard.
And he told us the factory is going to be moved, Plaszow is being liquidated.
We are going to be sent back to Plaszow, and then we're going to come to Czechoslovakia.
In order to make sure that we would all come, we were tattooed.
We were tattooed with KL, for Konzentration Lager.
And it was done by the Jewish doctors in the camps.
So, you know, they didn't do that deep, and it disappeared.
There's hardly just, just a few little spots on my skin are left.
And so we were sent back to Plaszow at that time.
There was hardly anybody left because Plaszow was liquidated in August.
The men and the women who are working in Plaszow were working in the different workshops.
They were all sent-- the men were sent to Mauthausen, to Gross-Rosen, to Mauthausen, and the woman were sent to Auschwitz.
The few people that were left-- Amon Goth was not there anymore.
The few people that were left, we work in a warehouse packing for Germany in boxes, cartons, in suitcase, in trucks gold, silver, brass, paintings, clothing, furs.
And they would ship everything that they plundered.
Everything the Germans stole, they brought it to Plaszow and we were packing it.
I think we were there maybe three weeks, maybe four weeks.
And then when it was-- when the job was done, we were going to be sent to-- we sold to Brunnlitz, to Czechoslovakia.
And there were still-- I don't know how many of us were.
I think maybe there were 2,000 women left in Plaszow.
But at one time, you know, when Plaszow started, there were like 18,000, sometimes went up to 20,000.
No, there were no men already when we came back.
All the men were sent away.
Maybe there were just a few.
I don't really remember.
And--
A lot of the men had remained in the factory to close up the, the factory.
And the rest of them, I said-- yeah, the rest of them came to, to Plaszow.
Then they were sent away before we.
They went first.
And so the morning that we were supposed to leave, we stood in front of the trains, trains and they called our name.
And for some reason, as I was walking to right after my mother so I would be on the same train with her-- I was supposed to go to another train.
I didn't know, you know.
And the SS guard woman grabbed me and beat me up because I wanted to go with my mother to the same train.
I didn't know I wasn't supposed to.
And then when we were put on a train, you know, it was--
No, my mother was on a separate train.
My mother was not-- I mean, the same train in another--
Car.
Car.
Because I know what happened.
After they-- after-- there were also some women that were, you know, there were other women, not just the Schindler's women.
And so I was separated from my mother.
And I was petrified.
But I was in another car with a cousin of mine.
And she said, don't worry, don't worry, I'll take care of you.
You don't have to worry.
And we were, we were so-- we were packed in the train.
It was so-- we were so-- the train, the car was so full that I remember that we couldn't turn around, couldn't move.
We had no water.
We had no bathroom facilities.
I think there was one little bucket in a corner.
You couldn't get to it.
There was no air.
There were no windows.
I mean, the little windows were blocked with cardboard or something so we couldn't see.
It was a nightmare.
It was a nightmare, and we didn't know where we were going.
It seemed like an awful long time.
Probably, probably about four or five hours.
But you know, it's only an hour from Krakow to Auschwitz now.
It's not, it's not that far.
In that time, it was a very long time.
And then, when the train stopped and we were told to get up, get out, you know, the gate slid open and had to jump down.
And we looked, and there in front of us is the miles of the barb wire.
And there is a sign, Auschwitz-Birkenau.
First of all, I started to call for my mother.
And I heard her calling me.
And so we ran to each other and then we were together.
And I remember, I look at my mother and I smell.
There was such a terrible smell in Auschwitz.
There was such stench in Auschwitz.
You could smell the burning flesh.
And there was smoke.
There was such heavy smoke in Auschwitz.
And you could see, you could, even at night, you could see the chimneys.
You could see the heavy, thick smoke coming from the chimney.
And it was like everything was covered with the, the smoke, with the ashes.
We were covered.
The barracks were covered.
The roads were covered.
Everything was covered.
And we were told to run, because you never walked.
You always had to run.
And we ran.
And we got stopped in front of a barrack.
It said on it, bath house.
And we were pushed into the barracks.
And there we stood in line.
We were told to strip, put everything on a bench, that we would come back for it.
And I put my shoes.
I had shoes.
I had whatever, a sweater.
It was cold, you know, winter, my clothes.
I had a little picture of my father that I carried under my tongue.
I wanted to save it.
And I was afraid-- they were looking in your mouth for gold teeth-- they would look.
So I took this little, this picture and put it in my shoe, thinking I will come back for it.
And they shaved us.
Men who stood naked, were shaved our hair, were shaved here.
So we're so humiliated.
I felt so dehumanized, like worse than cows.
I always thought the Germans were so kind to their animals.
And they were-- you know, when they took their animals on a train, on the boxcars, they made sure they had water and food and air.
When they walked with their dogs, they always gave them money.
But for us, it was nothing.
Nothing.
And then they-- there was woman working in that bath house, and somebody my mother knew.
And the woman told my mother my father was brought to Auschwitz and my father went to the gas chambers.
And there she told us all the children, the children that we were hiding in Auschwitz-- in, in Plaszow-- I never told you about the children, that we were hiding, you know.
The children that were smuggled to Plaszow from the ghetto, some of his parents gave their children away to Polish people.
But there were still 300 children in Plaszow, and the children had to be hidden.
At all times, they had to be hidden, because during the day, people were working or the night shift was sleeping, so they couldn't make any noise.
And the patrols would come into the barracks during the day to make sure nobody was there.
So little children had to hide on top of the bunk beds, and they had to be quiet.
There were always two people in each barrack, men in men's barracks, women's barracks.
They had to be, like, in charge, make sure everything is clean, everybody went to work.
And if somebody didn't go to work and didn't have a permit to stay, they had to report them.
So there were, like, two adults in each barrack, men and women.
And children were hidden, mostly, in women's barrack with their mothers.
Only if their mothers were dead would they stay with their father.
And there were some fathers that had little girls in the barracks cut their hair short so they would pass for boys.
So the children were always-- we always tried to save the children and hide the children and have extra food for the children.
And the children, they were so smart that they knew they couldn't play, they couldn't run.
They had to be quiet at all times, and they understood that their life depended on them.
And then one day, when we were standing on the upper platz, the loudspeaker announced that children were being rounded up.
The upper platz was, like, in a meadow, you know, in a valley.
And we could see the children being rounded up and put on the trucks.
And the children were crying.
They probably knew, and they just let us believe, or somebody told, that they found out.
And so the loudspeaker announced that if anybody, you know, would move, the children would be shot.
And we stood.
There were, like, probably 18,000 people.
And people started to move.
And when they heard that the children would be shot, they stopped.
And I remember then the loudspeaker started to play lullabies, children lullaby, and children was being taken away.
And we just stood there.
And people started to whisper.
Woman said, well, what can they do with those children?
They'll probably put them in orphans' home run by nuns.
And I remember thinking, how will we find them?
If my little cousin will survive, how would I find her after the war?
So when we got to-- so that's what we believed, that the children were taken to the orphan's home.
And it was after the children were taken away that my mother and I went to work for Oskar Schindler.
So when we came to Auschwitz in the bath house, the woman who knew my mother told her that the children, the 300 children from Plaszow, were brought to the gas chamber.
And you could see where we were in the bath house, which looks just like the gas chamber, you could see people walking to the gas chambers from other trains.
Hungarian-- all the Hungarian Jews came to the Auschwitz in 1944.
In April 1945, they were liberated.
The Russians came to, you know, to Hungary, to Poland.
So the Hungarian people were killed just was [INAUDIBLE] two weeks, three weeks away.
But that's how we found out that my father was killed and the children were killed.
And then after the showers, you know, they pushed us into another room.
We couldn't go back to get our clothes.
And in that room, there was a-- it was a big room with women guards.
And they were so sadistic.
They were so cruel.
They were beating us.
We were wet, we were cold, and there was a pile of clothes on one side and a pile of wooden shoes.
And I remember just pulling something that was like silk.
Nothing fitted us.
It was either too small for us or too big for us.
And the shoes, the wooden shoes.
It was cold.
It was September when we got to Auschwitz.
It was September.
Winter-- it's already winter cold in Poland.
And we walked through the streets in Auschwitz.
The roads of the camp, so muddy.
And we saw the people, the women, didn't look human, you know.
When they shaved our hair, when they shaved my hair and I look at my mother and all the other people I knew so well, I said, that can't be us, you know.
We are already dead.
We, we can't be alive anymore.
We were so, so totally dehumanized.
We were so humiliated and, and traumatized.
I mean, there was just-- I can't imagine, like, they took my soul out.
That's how I felt.
Auschwitz was such a hell.
I mean, there was never enough food, of course.
And we slept in the barracks where we had nothing, just on a wooden, on the wooden bunk beds or on the stone floors.
And I will go-- every morning they would take me and some others girls, and we would walk to the kitchen.
I had, like, a pole on my-- a wooden pole with the barrels on each side.
Of course, I could hardly carry it.
So by the time I came back from the kitchen, like half of it would spill.
And then we got to the barrack, people had to share utensils.
We didn't have enough utensils.
We had nothing.
We were-- I mean, every day, we were getting sicker and sicker.
But one day while we were standing, waiting again to be counted, Mengele came by and took me and another girl.
And I had no idea what we were going to go, what was going to happen to us.
And they took us, they walked us to another camp.
The soldiers walked us to, to a little clinic.
And the nurse told us to lay down on white beds.
And they took blood from us.
Took blood from me, blood from my friend.
Said they have to make plasma to send to the throne to save a German soldier.
She was so nice to us, that nurse.
And she gave each one of us a slice of bread with a slice of liverwurst.
And I remember I ate half of it right away and brought the other half to my mother.
But the terror I felt of how I was going to find my barrack, you know, how was I going to find my mother.
Because each time that I had to go away, they took me someplace.
I was always afraid I'll come back and I won't find my mother.
But of course, you know, I did.
Most of our time we spent by working.
And we were in, in--
Cleaning the streets.
We were, we were in Auschwitz for three weeks, the women on Oskar Schindler's ; And I think they took us out of there in the last minute before we all died.
And when they put us--
I don't know if they had no intention to send us to Oskar Schindler or if it was an honest mistake.
I don't know.

But of course Oskar Schindler, when we didn't come, when he found out we all went to Auschwitz, he sent somebody to Auschwitz with diamonds to bribe Rudolf Hoss, the commander of Auschwitz, and to get his women out.
And so they send us back on a train.
And we still didn't know where we were going.
But the woman guard that went with us seemed very happy, because they knew we were going to Brunnlitz.
And so when the train stopped after a long journey, one of my girlfriends got sick.
No, we were sitting down in a boxcar this time.
We were not so crowded.
And my girlfriend was laying on my lap.
And she was very sick.
She had a high fever.
She broke up with scarlet fever.
And I was catching-- this time we had a little window.
And I was taking snowflakes and icicles and putting it on her lips.
If we stayed in Auschwitz another hour, she would have broken up in scarlet fever, they would never have let us go.
We would have been sent straight to the gas chamber.
Herte.
Herte.
And then, you know, the train stopped and the doors opened.

And there stood Oskar Schindler, just, just the way you saw, that you saw the movie, just the way Oskar Schindler was with his green Tyrolean hat with the feather in the hat and the cigar in his mouth.
And he stood there and he said, you are finally here, and I'll take care of you.
And you have nothing to worry about.
And of course, he took care of us.
He risked his life.
He risked his fortune.
He asked Mrs.
Schindler to come to Brunnlitz, and she did.
And she helped take care of the sick.
She used to go to the village and try to get some extra food for the sick.
They got some farina for us, for the young ones like me.
And she cooked the farina.
Every day for two weeks we walked across the factory floor to-- they had a little apartment off the factory floor.
They had a beautiful villa.
And they never slept in the villa, because we were afraid when they would be away from us that we would be killed by the guards.
So they always stayed.
They never went away together.
If he went away, she would stay.
And of course, Oskar Schindler was arrested twice.
And he was released because he had such high connections.
And Mr.
Schindler knew exactly who to call to help.
Gestapo.
Who were the guards in Brunnlitz?
One was, you know, SS.
There was SS.
His name was-- the commander was Liepold.
Liepold wanted to kill us in the worst way.
He hated Oskar Schindler.
You know why he loved him?
Because they always-- you know, he was bribed with food and money and jewelry and all kinds of things, and whiskey.
But Oskar Schindler played a trick on Liepold.
They-- one of our guys made--
